title: Minimally Destructive Fast Imaging of Single Atoms in an Optical Tweezer Array with Coherent Excitation
authors: Rei Yokoyama, Takumi Kashimoto, Kosuke Shibata, Yuki Kawamura, Toshi Kusano, Chih-Han Yeh, Reiji Asano, Yuma Nakamura, Tetsushi Takano, Yosuke Takasu, Yoshiro Takahashi
abstract: Ultracold neutral atoms in an optical lattice and an optical tweezer array offer highly-controllable quantum many-body systems, utilized for various quantum science and technology such as quantum computing, quantum metrology, and quantum simulation. By combining high-fidelity imaging of individual atoms, one can further enhance the capability of such experimental platforms as quantum gas microscopes, tweezer clocks, and tweezer-array-based quantum computers. In this work, we propose a minimally destructive single-atom imaging by deterministic coherent excitation of atoms with alternately applied pi-pulses from counter-propagating directions, mitigating the fundamental heating effect associated with the stochastic absorption process. Using ytterbium-174 atoms trapped in an optical tweezer array, we experimentally demonstrate fast and low-loss single-atom imaging with a discrimination fidelity of 99.89(5) % and a survival probability of 98.80(44) % in 17.6 microseconds. Importantly, our scheme exhibits the lower heating rate, about half of that of the former scheme utilizing the incoherent excitation. This fast and minimally destructive imaging scheme is beneficial for relaxing the requirement on the trap depth, thereby enabling scalable atom imaging across a wide range of quantum science platforms.
summary: This paper presents a new method for imaging single atoms in optical tweezers using coherent pi-pulses instead of traditional incoherent excitation. This approach significantly reduces heating and allows for extremely fast imaging in the microsecond regime. Such a technique is crucial for scaling up quantum computers based on neutral atom arrays by enabling faster error correction and higher-fidelity measurements.
main_problem: Traditional fluorescence imaging of single atoms in optical tweezers causes significant heating due to the stochastic nature of photon absorption, which limits imaging speed and atom survival. The goal is to develop a minimally destructive, microsecond-scale imaging scheme that reduces heating.
main_result: The authors demonstrated a coherent excitation scheme using alternating pi-pulses that achieves a 99.89% imaging fidelity and a 98.80% survival probability in just 17.6 microseconds. This method reduces the heating rate to approximately half of that seen in traditional incoherent excitation schemes.
method: The researchers used deterministic coherent excitation via a sequence of 1.1 ns-long pi-pulses from counter-propagating directions using a 399 nm UV laser. They employed a release-and-recapture method and an adiabatic trap-depth ramp-down technique to measure atomic temperature and heating rates.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of Ytterbium-154 atoms trapped in a 2D optical tweezer array using 532 nm light. The imaging utilizes the 1S0 to 1P1 transition at 399 nm.
key_observables: Photon counts (integrated fluorescence), discrimination fidelity, survival probability, atomic temperature, and heating rate per photon.
important_parameters: Imaging duration of 17.6 microseconds, pi-pulse width of 1.1 ns, trap depth of 2.27 mK, and a 399 nm transition wavelength.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the fluorescence per pulse is independent (atom starts in ground state) and uses a 1D model for the physics of atom escape from the trap.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup and pi-pulse sequence; Fig 2 shows the linear dependence of photons on pulse number and the effect of pulse intervals on survival; Fig 3 compares heating rates and temperature evolution for coherent vs. incoherent schemes; Fig 4 shows calculated loss probability.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of heating in atom imaging, describes the experimental setup and the proposed coherent pi-pulse scheme, presents results confirming coherent excitation via Rabi oscillations, compares heating rates between coherent and incoherent methods, investigates potential heating mechanisms like Dipole Force Fluctuation, and concludes with the implications for scalable quantum technologies.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and AMO physics as it provides a way to bypass the fundamental heating limits of stochastic photon absorption, enabling much faster readout and higher-fidelity control in neutral atom quantum computing platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Imaging of a 174Yb atom array with coherent excitation. (a) Schematic of the experimental setup and pulse sequence. An array of 174Yb atoms is addressed by a series of π-pulses resonant with the 1S0 ↔1P1 399 nm transition at an intensity I ≫Isat at intervals of tint. The spontaneously emitted photons are collected by a high NA = 0.6 and imaged onto an EM-CCD camera. Relevant energy levels and transition of 174Yb are shown on the upper left. (b) Observation of Rabi oscillations via fluorescence detection as a function of pulse width. The experimental data are represented by circles and the error bars are the 1-σ standard error of the mean. The fit of the data with the semiclassical...

FIG. 2. Systematic investigation of the coherent excitation scheme. (a) Number of emitted photons measured as a function of the number of pulses. The detected photon counts are converted into the number of spontaneously emitted photons using the estimated photon collection efficiency of our imaging setup. The experimental data are represented by circles. A linear fit yields a emission rate of 0.90(2) photons per pulse. (b,c) Dependence of the emitted photon count per pulse (b) and normalized survival probability (c) on the pulse interval, using a sequence of 800 pulses. Suppression in both signals at shorter intervals indicates that stimulated emission occurs before spontaneous decay,...

FIG. 3. Heating rates during imaging with coherent and incoherent excitation schemes. (a) Evolution of the atomic temperature as a function of the number of pulses, measured via the release-and-recapture method. Number of photon emission is extracted from detected counts by accounting for photon collection efficiency. The data with blue and red circles represent the results in the cases of coherent and incoherent excitation at the trap depth of 2.27 mK, respectively. For the coherent excitation scheme, the experiments are also performed at trap depths of 1.45 mK (blue square) and 0.74 mK (blue triangle) to investigate the effect of DFF. Inset: A representative measurement with the...

FIG. 4. Calculated loss probability as a function of number of photon emission with initial atomic temperature of 30 µK and trap depth of 2.27 mK. The blue (red) line represents the result of imaging with coherent (incoherent) excitation scheme.

FIG. 5. Optical setup for the short and intense probe beams. See text for the basic configuration. The Intensity Modulators (IM) generate the sub-nano second pulses for 798 nm light through the pulse input with the voltage Vπ corresponding to the π-pulse from the RF ports, whereas the DC ports are used for the bias control to minimize leakage light. The shutters are placed before the input for the optical fibers. AOM, TA, RF, DC, SHG, and PD represents acousto-optic modulators, tapered-amplifiers, radio-frequency, direct current, second-harmonic-generation, and photo-detectors, respectively.
